critique.summary: The article discusses a proposed change in C++26 to deprecate ellipsis parameters without a preceding comma, aiming to improve compatibility with C, reduce confusion with template parameter packs, and enable future language features. The change mandates a comma before the ellipsis in function parameters, similar to the Oxford comma in English lists.
mainPoints: C++ currently allows ellipsis parameters both with and without a preceding comma, but C only allows the comma form.
The comma-less ellipsis parameter syntax causes confusion, especially with template parameter packs introduced in C++11.
C++26 will deprecate ellipsis parameters without a preceding comma, requiring code to use the comma form for compatibility and clarity.
The deprecation is non-breaking and can be automated by tooling to add the comma where missing.
This change preserves design space for future language features and improves consistency with C.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the proposal P3176R1 to deprecate ellipsis parameters without a preceding comma in C++26. It correctly explains the historical context, the difference between C and C++ syntax, and the confusion caused by the comma-less form especially in relation to template parameter packs. The explanation of the impact and rationale aligns with publicly available information from the ISO C++ committee papers and discussions. The article is authored by Sandor Dargo, a known C++ expert, lending credibility to the technical details. However, the article does not cite multiple sources or perspectives, focusing mainly on the proposal author's viewpoint.
considerations: The article does not provide perspectives from other C++ committee members or the broader C++ community.
It does not discuss potential challenges or resistance to adopting the new syntax in existing large codebases.
The article does not mention any timeline for when the deprecation warnings will be enforced or if removal is planned beyond deprecation.
It omits discussion of how tooling support (compilers, linters) will handle this change in practice.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the complexity and backward compatibility of C++ syntax, particularly regarding variadic templates and deprecated features. Some commenters appreciate efforts to simplify the language, while others express frustration with its increasing complexity and prefer alternatives like C, Rust, or .NET.

comments.author: advael
comments.text: This seems pretty good to me just on the level of trying to read C as someone using C++. Parameter packs and variadic templates are easily the most confusing syntax in C++ and cleaning it up is... very welcome
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the complexity of variadic templates in C++ and welcomes improvements, reflecting a knowledgeable and constructive perspective.

comments.author: staplung
comments.text:
Of course since the old syntax is merely deprecated and not removed, going forward you now have to know the old, bad form and the new, good form in order to read code. Backwards compatibility is a strength but also a one-way complexity ratchet.

At least they managed to kill `auto_ptr`.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses the trade-off between backward compatibility and increased complexity, a well-known issue in language evolution.

comments.author: HackerThemAll
comments.text: Yes, but no. I learned C++ in '90s when it was C with classes and some other noise added by Stroustrup. During the some 25 years that followed it had became a mess that's insanely hard to work with. I'm not going back to this language. I prefer plain C or Rust, leaning towards Rust when I fully comprehend the lifetime and borrow checker. Or when I have the luxury of having a GCed runtime, then the .NET with its easiest C# language with wonderful abundance of great libraries is the best choice. Nobody was ever fired for using .NET (for right purposes).
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a subjective but valid viewpoint on C++'s increasing complexity and preference for other languages, reflecting a common sentiment among some developers.

comments.author: mFixman
comments.analysis: This comment shares a specific technical anecdote about undefined behavior in C++ related to variadic functions, which is accurate but niche and less relevant to the main discussion.

comments.author: rusakov-field
comments.text:
Clarity and Elegance of Syntax > Backwards compatibility.

In my opinion anyway. C++ feels so bloated these days.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a personal opinion favoring clarity over backward compatibility, a common debate in language design, though it is subjective.

comments.author: zlfn
comments.text: C++ seems to be constantly getting complicated. If the major version were to change, there wouldn't be any need for backward compatibility with the existing code, and it would have been okay to delete that syntax while creating an automatic formatter.
comments.analysis: This comment proposes a hypothetical solution to complexity by breaking backward compatibility, which is a reasonable but debatable stance.

comments.author: throwaway2027
comments.text: C++ got too complicated after C++23 I went back to C.
comments.analysis: A brief personal reaction expressing frustration with C++ complexity and a return to C; accurate as a subjective experience but lacks detail.

comments.author: blueaquilae
comments.text:
I'm far from C++ but reading this article confused be, from the form to the impact to the dead link https://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg21/docs/papers/2021/p12...

I guess that's a preview how C++ require a lifelong commitment.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects confusion and a beginner's perspective, which is valid but adds little analytical value to the discussion.

comments.author: lasgawe
comments.text: learned something new. thanks for the article.
comments.analysis: A polite but non-analytical comment that does not contribute to the technical or thematic discussion.
